news_summary: Recent breakthroughs reveal how the flu virus invades cells, the lasting brain benefits of magic mushrooms, a food compound that boosts vaccine effectiveness in older adults, and promising advances against cancer-related cachexia.
news_summary_fr: Des découvertes récentes révèlent comment le virus de la grippe envahit les cellules, les bienfaits durables des champignons hallucinogènes sur le cerveau, un composé alimentaire qui renforce l'efficacité des vaccins chez les personnes âgées, ainsi que des avancées prometteuses dans la lutte contre la cachexie liée au cancer.
news_summary_es: Los últimos descubrimientos revelan cómo el virus de la gripe invade las células, los beneficios duraderos para el cerebro de las setas alucinógenas, un compuesto alimentario que potencia la eficacia de las vacunas en las personas mayores y avances prometedores contra la caquexia relacionada con el cáncer.

articles.title: New molecular map reveals how the flu virus hijacks human cells
articles.summary: Scientists uncovered how influenza A commandeers human cells, including a surprising strategy that dissolves tiny structures in the nucleus and releases proteins the virus may use to reproduce. The detailed molecular map could reveal new drug targets and help researchers study dangerous flu strains such as H5N1.
articles.content:
Researchers at EMBL Hamburg, working with scientists at the Leibniz Research Institute for Molecular Pharmacology (FMP), have produced an unusually detailed map of how influenza A reshapes infected human cells. Their customized workflow allowed them to observe protein interactions directly inside intact cells rather than relying only on samples that had been broken apart.

Seasonal influenza causes 3-5 million cases of severe illness worldwide each year and is linked to as many as 650,000 deaths. Influenza A has also driven multiple pandemics, including the 1918 Spanish Flu pandemic.

After entering a cell, the virus releases RNA containing the instructions for making a small set of viral proteins. Those proteins spread through the host cell and redirect its molecular systems, turning the cell into a production site for new virus particles.

Mapping the Flu Virus Inside Intact Cells

A clearer view of this takeover could help scientists develop more effective flu vaccines and antiviral drugs. To reach that goal, researchers need to know which viral proteins interact with human proteins, where those contacts occur, and how the virus uses them to support its own replication.

The new study is the first to map direct contacts between influenza proteins and human proteins on a large scale inside intact infected cells. The structural detail was also precise enough for researchers to model how the interacting proteins may fit together.

"Our work provides a new way to study flu-host interactions in their native context and with structural insight," said Jan Kosinski, Group Leader at EMBL Hamburg and Centre for Structural Systems Biology (CSSB). "The current results are a snapshot of a moment during infection, and it opens the door to studying flu-host interactions across the entire infection cycle."

Overcoming a Major Experimental Problem

Tracking protein-protein interactions during an active infection is extremely difficult. Many earlier studies depended on biochemical techniques that required scientists to break cells open before measuring protein contacts.

That process can distort what was happening inside the living cell. Once internal compartments are destroyed, proteins that were originally separated may come into contact in the laboratory. At the same time, weak, temporary, or location-specific interactions can disappear. As a result, researchers may struggle to determine which connections truly existed during infection.

"This is when we learned that our collaborators, Boris Bogdanow and Fan Liu, at FMP Berlin had developed a specialized version of cross-linking mass spectrometry (XL-MS), a long-established technique for mapping protein contacts, tailored specifically to virus-infected cells," said Kosinski.

The specialized method provided the breakthrough the team needed. It made it possible to capture interactions that occur only briefly or within particular regions of an infected cell.

"XL-MS allows us to capture protein-protein interactions directly in infected intact cells, while also providing structural information about how these interactions are happening," explained Bogdanow, who is now a Junior Research Group Leader at the Institute of Virology, Charité -- Universitätsmedizin Berlin. "This gives us insight into the interface between the virus and the human cell and may, through structural modeling, help identify actionable targets for future pharmaceutical interventions."

Combining Experimental Data With AlphaFold

The researchers paired their XL-MS results with computational structural modeling. This allowed them to identify viral and human proteins that interact and estimate how those proteins are positioned when they connect.

To build these structural models, the team used a modified version of AlphaFold, the Nobel prize-winning protein structure prediction algorithm.

"The key advantage of the modified AlphaFold approach is that it allowed us to feed our experimental cross-linking data directly into the structural modeling," explained Kosinski. "This tells the model which parts of the viral and host proteins are close to each other inside infected cells. This was especially useful for virus-host complexes, which are often difficult to predict reliably."

Two Ways Influenza Hijacks Human Cells

The findings, published in Nature Microbiology, revealed two notable strategies that influenza A appears to use when taking control of a cell.

The first centers on hemagglutinin, a protein found on the virus's surface. Influenza uses hemagglutinin to attach to and enter host cells. The researchers followed this protein as it traveled through the cell's internal transport and processing network.

This network consists of compartments that fold, modify, and prepare proteins before sending them to their final locations. The analysis showed that several human proteins helped fold and modify hemagglutinin correctly during infection. Some of those host proteins previously had poorly understood functions.

Influenza Dissolves Structures in the Nucleus

The second discovery involved paraspeckles, small droplet-like compartments located inside the cell nucleus. The team found that influenza A infection caused these structures to dissolve.

When paraspeckles broke apart, they released RNA-binding proteins that had been held inside them. The virus may then use those proteins to support its own replication.

"What surprised us most was the paraspeckles," said Iuliia Kotova, former predoctoral fellow at the Kosinski Group at EMBL Hamburg, currently at ETH Zurich and first author of the publication. "Watching these tiny organelles in the nucleus dissolve, consistently across every cell line and every flu strain we tested, told us this isn't a side effect of infection -- it might be a strategy,"

The disruption may provide influenza with more than one advantage.

"There may also be a second benefit for the virus: some evidence suggests paraspeckles contribute to cellular stress responses and antiviral gene regulation, so disrupting them could also weaken parts of the cell's defense response," added Kosinski.

A Collaborative Effort Across Three Institutions

The project depended on shared technology and expertise from three institutions. Researchers carried out the cross-linking mass spectrometry work at Charité in Berlin. Glycoproteomics analyses were completed at the EMBL Proteomics Core Facility.

The team performed AlphaFold modeling on the EMBL Compute Cluster, while microscopy imaging took place at CSSB's Advanced Light and Fluorescence Microscopy (ALFM) Facility.

A New Way to Study Potential Pandemic Viruses

The findings demonstrate how studying molecular contacts inside intact infected cells can reveal both where and how a virus takes control of human cellular machinery. The researchers say this type of 'mapping in context' may also help explain how other viruses operate.

"While the exact host factors and mechanisms often differ from virus to virus, we think our overall approach -- combining in-cell cross-linking, structural modeling, and targeted cell-biology follow-up to map native virus-host interactions at specific stages of infection -- remains broadly applicable," Kosinski said.

Although the study examined a laboratory-adapted strain of influenza, the researchers believe the same strategy could eventually be used to investigate viruses with greater pandemic potential.

Bogdanow agrees: "Although this study has focused on a lab-adapted strain, this study lays the groundwork to apply the methodology to viruses of potential pandemic relevance, such as H5N1, and for uncovering the interaction networks that support their multiplication in human cells."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in understanding how influenza A virus hijacks human cells, using advanced molecular mapping and computational modeling. This discovery has broad implications for developing more effective flu vaccines and antiviral drugs, potentially benefiting millions worldwide by improving flu treatment and pandemic preparedness. The study is detailed, focused, and demonstrates a novel method with wide applicability to other viruses, fulfilling criteria for positive real-world impact, significance, and substance.
articles.personality_title: Scientists map how flu virus takes over human cells in new detail
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Every year, seasonal flu causes millions of serious illnesses and hundreds of thousands of deaths worldwide. The influenza A virus is especially dangerous, having caused major pandemics in the past. To fight it better, scientists need to understand exactly how this virus hijacks human cells.

**What happened** – Researchers from EMBL Hamburg and other institutes created a detailed map showing how influenza A interacts with proteins inside living human cells. They used a special method called cross-linking mass spectrometry (XL-MS) that lets them see protein contacts without breaking the cells apart. Combining this with computer modeling using AlphaFold, they identified two key ways the virus takes control: it uses human proteins to help shape its own proteins, and it breaks down tiny structures in the cell nucleus called paraspeckles, releasing proteins that may help the virus reproduce.

**Impact** – This is the first time scientists have seen these detailed interactions inside intact infected cells. Understanding these steps shows new targets for drugs or vaccines to stop the flu virus. The discovery about paraspeckles is especially new and may explain how the virus weakens the cell’s defenses while using its resources to multiply.

**What's next step** – The researchers plan to apply this mapping approach to study other flu strains, including dangerous ones like H5N1. They hope their method will help find new ways to stop flu and other viruses by showing exactly how they hijack human cells at different infection stages.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new detailed map reveals how influenza A virus reshapes human cells, offering fresh clues to fight flu infections and future pandemics.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques cartographient en détail comment le virus de la grippe prend le contrôle des cellules humaines
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Chaque année, la grippe saisonnière cause des millions de maladies graves et des centaines de milliers de décès dans le monde. Le virus de la grippe A est particulièrement dangereux, ayant provoqué de grandes pandémies dans le passé. Pour mieux le combattre, les scientifiques doivent comprendre comment ce virus prend le contrôle des cellules humaines.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’EMBL Hambourg et d’autres instituts ont créé une carte détaillée montrant comment le virus de la grippe A interagit avec les protéines à l’intérieur des cellules humaines vivantes. Ils ont utilisé une méthode spéciale appelée spectrométrie de masse par réticulation croisée (XL-MS) qui permet de voir les contacts entre protéines sans casser les cellules. En combinant cela avec la modélisation informatique AlphaFold, ils ont identifié deux façons clés dont le virus prend le contrôle : il utilise des protéines humaines pour aider à former ses propres protéines, et il détruit de petites structures dans le noyau de la cellule appelées paraspeckles, libérant des protéines qui pourraient aider le virus à se reproduire.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois que les scientifiques observent ces interactions détaillées à l’intérieur de cellules infectées intactes. Comprendre ces étapes montre de nouvelles cibles pour des médicaments ou vaccins pour arrêter le virus de la grippe. La découverte des paraspeckles est particulièrement nouvelle et pourrait expliquer comment le virus affaiblit les défenses de la cellule tout en utilisant ses ressources pour se multiplier.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs prévoient d’appliquer cette méthode de cartographie à d’autres souches de grippe, y compris des souches dangereuses comme H5N1. Ils espèrent que leur méthode aidera à trouver de nouvelles façons d’arrêter la grippe et d’autres virus en montrant exactement comment ils prennent le contrôle des cellules humaines à différents stades de l’infection.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une nouvelle carte détaillée révèle comment le virus de la grippe A transforme les cellules humaines, offrant de nouveaux indices pour lutter contre la grippe et les futures pandémies.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos mapean en detalle cómo el virus de la gripe controla las células humanas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Cada año, la gripe estacional causa millones de enfermedades graves y cientos de miles de muertes en todo el mundo. El virus de la influenza A es especialmente peligroso, habiendo provocado grandes pandemias en el pasado. Para combatirlo mejor, los científicos necesitan entender cómo este virus toma el control de las células humanas.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores del EMBL Hamburgo y otros institutos crearon un mapa detallado que muestra cómo la influenza A interactúa con proteínas dentro de células humanas vivas. Usaron un método especial llamado espectrometría de masas con entrecruzamiento (XL-MS) que permite ver contactos entre proteínas sin romper las células. Combinando esto con modelado computacional usando AlphaFold, identificaron dos formas clave en que el virus toma control: usa proteínas humanas para ayudar a formar sus propias proteínas y disuelve pequeñas estructuras en el núcleo celular llamadas paraspeckles, liberando proteínas que podrían ayudar al virus a reproducirse.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que los científicos ven estas interacciones detalladas dentro de células infectadas intactas. Entender estos pasos muestra nuevos objetivos para medicamentos o vacunas que detengan el virus de la gripe. El descubrimiento sobre los paraspeckles es especialmente nuevo y puede explicar cómo el virus debilita las defensas de la célula mientras usa sus recursos para multiplicarse.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean aplicar este enfoque de mapeo para estudiar otras cepas de gripe, incluyendo las peligrosas como H5N1. Esperan que su método ayude a encontrar nuevas formas de detener la gripe y otros virus mostrando exactamente cómo toman el control de las células humanas en diferentes etapas de la infección.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Un nuevo mapa detallado revela cómo el virus de la influenza A transforma las células humanas, ofreciendo nuevas pistas para combatir la gripe y futuras pandemias.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a delicate, glowing network of intertwined protein strands inside a translucent, stylized human cell, showing vibrant virus-like shapes gently interacting and merging with human protein structures, alongside softly dissolving droplet-like compartments within a glowing nucleus, all rendered with natural, soft colors and fine, intricate lines to symbolize the flu virus’s subtle takeover of cellular machinery.

articles.title: Magic mushrooms may reshape the brain long after the trip ends
articles.summary: A single dose of psilocybin produced measurable changes in brain activity and possible changes in brain structure that lasted for up to a month. Participants who experienced the greatest increase in flexible, varied brain activity also reported more personal insight and later improvements in well-being. The findings suggest that the psychedelic experience itself may help people break free from entrenched patterns of thinking.
articles.content:
A single dose of psilocybin, the psychedelic substance in magic mushrooms, may produce physical changes in the brain that remain detectable for as long as a month, according to researchers at UC San Francisco and Imperial College London.

The study, published in Nature Communications, involved healthy adults who had never previously used a psychedelic. Although none of the participants had a diagnosed mental health condition, the findings may offer clues about why psilocybin has shown therapeutic potential for depression, anxiety, and addiction.

Researchers focused on temporary increases in brain "entropy," a term describing the variety and unpredictability of neural activity. Their results suggest that this more diverse pattern of brain activity may promote psychological insight and that the psychedelic experience itself could play an important role in psilocybin's longer-term effects.

A larger rise in entropy during the minutes and hours after a high dose was associated with greater emotional insight the following day. Participants who reported more insight were also more likely to describe improved well-being one month later.

"Psychedelic means 'psyche-revealing,' or making the psyche visible," said senior author Robin Carhart-Harris, PhD, the Ralph Metzner Distinguished Professor of Neurology at UCSF. "Our data shows that such experiences of psychological insight relate to an entropic quality of brain activity and how both are involved in causing subsequent improvements in mental health. It suggests that the trip -- and its correlates in the brain -- is a key component of how psychedelic therapy works."

Measuring Psilocybin's Effects on the Brain

The researchers used several methods to examine brain activity, connectivity, and structure. Measurements were taken before the psychedelic dose, during the peak of the experience, and again one month later.

Because none of the 28 volunteers had a diagnosed mental health condition, the team was able to perform a broader range of tests than might have been possible in a clinical patient group.

During the first stage of the experiment, participants received 1 mg of psilocybin, which the researchers treated as a placebo dose. Their brain activity was then monitored with electroencephalography (EEG), which records electrical signals through electrodes placed on the scalp.

Over the following weeks, the researchers evaluated psychological insight, well-being, and cognitive performance. They also used functional MRI (fMRI) to study brain activity and diffusion tensor imaging (DTI) to examine connections between different parts of the brain.

One month after receiving the placebo dose, the participants were given 25 mg of psilocybin, an amount capable of producing a powerful psychedelic experience. Researchers again recorded brain activity with EEG during the trip and repeated the same psychological and imaging assessments over the weeks that followed.

This design allowed the team to compare the effects of a full psychedelic dose with those observed after the 1 mg placebo.

Psilocybin Increased Brain Entropy

Within 60 minutes of taking the 25 mg dose, EEG recordings showed a rise in brain entropy. The finding suggests that the brain was generating and processing a broader range of information while under the influence of psilocybin.

When the researchers examined participants one month later with DTI, they found signs that neural tracts were denser and showed greater integrity. DTI works by measuring how water moves along pathways in the brain.

This pattern was the reverse of changes commonly associated with aging, when neural tracts tend to become more diffuse.

The researchers emphasized that additional studies are needed to determine exactly what these structural findings mean. Even so, they described the result as a previously unseen indication that psychedelics may produce lasting changes in the brain.

Lasting Gains in Insight and Well-Being

The day after taking 25 mg of psilocybin, 27 of the 28 participants described the experience as the "single most" unusual state of consciousness they had ever encountered. The remaining participant ranked it among the five most unusual experiences of their life.

Participants also reported substantially more psychological insight after the 25 mg dose than after the 1 mg placebo.

Measures of well-being improved two weeks and four weeks after the high dose. These assessments included responses to statements such as, "I've been feeling optimistic about the future" and "I've been dealing with problems well."

One month after the experiment, participants also performed better on a test of cognitive flexibility, which measures the ability to adapt thinking and respond to changing information.

"Psilocybin seems to loosen up stereotyped patterns of brain activity and give people the ability to revise entrenched patterns of thought," said Taylor Lyons, PhD, a research associate at Imperial College London and the first author of the paper. "The fact that these changes track with insight and improved well-being is especially exciting."

How the Psychedelic Experience May Drive Benefits

The participants with the largest increases in brain entropy during the first minutes and hours after taking psilocybin were also the most likely to report greater insight the next day.

Those increases in insight were then associated with stronger improvements in well-being one month later. The researchers concluded that the experience of psychological insight appeared to drive the later gains in well-being.

The findings could eventually help clinicians refine psilocybin treatments for people with mental health conditions. One possibility would be to identify a dose that produces enough brain entropy to encourage meaningful insight without creating an unnecessarily intense experience.

"We already knew psilocybin could be helpful for treating mental illness," Carhart-Harris said. "But now we have a much better understanding of how."

Authors: Other UCSF authors are Manesh Girn, PhD, Hannes Kettner, and Adam Gazzaley, MD, PhD. For all authors, see the paper.

Funding: This work was funded by philanthropic donations to the Centre for Psychedelic Research, Imperial College London, the Alex Mosley Charitable Trust, and the Beckley Foundation. Carhart-Harris is supported by the Ralph Metzner Distinguished Professorship and philanthropic donations to the Psychedelics Division, Neuroscape, UCSF, plus philanthropic donations to the Carhart-Harris Lab.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific study showing that a single dose of psilocybin produces lasting positive changes in brain activity and structure, linked to improved psychological insight and well-being. This has broad implications for mental health treatment, potentially benefiting many people with depression, anxiety, and addiction. The study is well-detailed, focused on a single topic, and presents meaningful, measurable impacts.
articles.personality_title: Single dose of magic mushrooms leads to lasting brain changes and better well-being
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have been studying psilocybin, the active ingredient in magic mushrooms, because it may help treat mental health problems like depression and anxiety. This study looked at how a single dose affects the brain and feelings over time in healthy adults.

**What happened** – Researchers at UC San Francisco and Imperial College London gave 28 volunteers two doses: a very small 1 mg dose as a placebo and later a full 25 mg dose of psilocybin. They used brain scans and tests before, during, and up to a month after the doses. After the full dose, brain activity became more varied and flexible, and brain connections looked stronger one month later. Participants also reported more personal insight and better well-being.

**Impact** – This study shows that one experience with psilocybin can change the brain’s activity and structure for weeks. These brain changes were linked to people feeling more optimistic and thinking more clearly. It suggests that the psychedelic experience helps break old ways of thinking, which could explain why psilocybin might work as a therapy.

**What's next step** – Scientists want to learn exactly how these brain changes happen and how to use psilocybin safely to help people with mental health issues. Future studies may find the right dose that improves insight without causing too strong a psychedelic experience.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A single dose of psilocybin can create lasting changes in the brain that improve personal insight and well-being in healthy adults.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une dose unique de champignons magiques modifie durablement le cerveau et améliore le bien-être
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient la psilocybine, l’ingrédient actif des champignons magiques, car elle pourrait aider à traiter des troubles mentaux comme la dépression et l’anxiété. Cette étude a examiné comment une seule dose affecte le cerveau et les sensations au fil du temps chez des adultes en bonne santé.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université de Californie à San Francisco et du Imperial College de Londres ont donné à 28 volontaires deux doses : une très faible dose de 1 mg comme placebo, puis une dose complète de 25 mg de psilocybine. Ils ont utilisé des scanners cérébraux et des tests avant, pendant et jusqu’à un mois après les doses. Après la dose complète, l’activité cérébrale est devenue plus variée et flexible, et les connexions cérébrales semblaient plus fortes un mois plus tard. Les participants ont aussi ressenti plus d’introspection personnelle et un meilleur bien-être.

**Impact** – Cette étude montre qu’une seule expérience avec la psilocybine peut changer l’activité et la structure du cerveau pendant des semaines. Ces changements cérébraux étaient liés à un sentiment d’optimisme et à une meilleure clarté de pensée. Cela suggère que l’expérience psychédélique aide à sortir de vieilles habitudes mentales, ce qui pourrait expliquer pourquoi la psilocybine fonctionne comme thérapie.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques veulent comprendre précisément comment ces changements cérébraux se produisent et comment utiliser la psilocybine en toute sécurité pour aider les personnes souffrant de troubles mentaux. Les futures études pourraient trouver la dose idéale qui améliore l’introspection sans provoquer une expérience psychédélique trop intense.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une seule dose de psilocybine peut provoquer des changements durables dans le cerveau qui améliorent l’introspection personnelle et le bien-être chez des adultes en bonne santé.
articles.personality_title_es: Una dosis única de hongos mágicos produce cambios duraderos en el cerebro y mejora el bienestar
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han estado estudiando la psilocibina, el ingrediente activo de los hongos mágicos, porque podría ayudar a tratar problemas de salud mental como la depresión y la ansiedad. Este estudio analizó cómo una sola dosis afecta el cerebro y las emociones con el tiempo en adultos sanos.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de California en San Francisco y del Imperial College de Londres dieron a 28 voluntarios dos dosis: una dosis muy baja de 1 mg como placebo y luego una dosis completa de 25 mg de psilocibina. Usaron escáneres cerebrales y pruebas antes, durante y hasta un mes después de las dosis. Después de la dosis completa, la actividad cerebral se volvió más variada y flexible, y las conexiones cerebrales se vieron más fuertes un mes después. Los participantes también reportaron mayor autoconocimiento y mejor bienestar.

**Impacto** – Este estudio muestra que una sola experiencia con psilocibina puede cambiar la actividad y estructura del cerebro durante semanas. Estos cambios cerebrales se relacionaron con que las personas se sintieran más optimistas y pensaran con más claridad. Sugiere que la experiencia psicodélica ayuda a romper viejos patrones de pensamiento, lo que podría explicar por qué la psilocibina funciona como terapia.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos quieren entender exactamente cómo ocurren estos cambios cerebrales y cómo usar la psilocibina de forma segura para ayudar a personas con problemas de salud mental. Estudios futuros podrían encontrar la dosis correcta que mejore el autoconocimiento sin causar una experiencia psicodélica demasiado intensa.

**Resumen en una frase** – Una sola dosis de psilocibina puede crear cambios duraderos en el cerebro que mejoran el autoconocimiento y el bienestar en adultos sanos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a glowing, intricately connected neural network shaped like a blooming mushroom, with vibrant, flowing energy lines symbolizing expanding brain activity and insight, set against a soft, natural background of gentle earth tones and subtle light bursts to evoke lasting positive transformation.

articles.title: This food compound may help vaccines work better in older adults
articles.summary: A naturally occurring compound found in foods such as wheat germ, mushrooms, and aged cheese may help older adults build stronger protection after vaccination. In a small pilot trial, researchers gave spermidine supplements to adults over 65 following their third COVID-19 vaccine dose. The biggest improvements appeared among people whose immune systems initially responded poorly, with higher antibody levels and stronger activity against several variants.
articles.content:
A naturally occurring compound called spermidine may help strengthen vaccine responses in some older adults by reducing biological signs of immune system aging, according to research published in Aging Cell.

The immune system becomes less efficient as people grow older, making it harder for the body to fight infections and respond fully to vaccines. This gradual decline, known as immunosenescence, can increase vulnerability to age-related health problems and leave some older adults with weaker protection after vaccination.

Spermidine May Support Aging Immunity

The research was led by Dr. Katja Simon, Group Leader of the Cell Biology of Immunity lab at the Max Delbrück Center, and Dr. Ghada Alsaleh, Associate Professor at the Nuffield Department of Orthopaedics, Rheumatology & Musculoskeletal Sciences of University of Oxford (NDORMS).

Their findings suggest that taking a daily spermidine supplement improved several measures of immune response following COVID-19 vaccination.

Spermidine is produced naturally by human cells and is also present in foods including wheat germ, mushrooms, parmesan, and cheddar. Earlier research has indicated that the molecule may support cellular maintenance mechanisms that become less active with age.

"Many older adults respond well to vaccines," Alsaleh explains. "But some do not develop strong protection, even after repeated vaccination. Biological aging of immune cells may be one reason why this happens. Our findings suggest that spermidine could help restore aspects of immune function in this group."

The project also involved researchers from the Oxford Vaccine Group, including Drs. Paul Klenerman, Teresa Lambe, and Lucy Jones, along with Owen B. Spiller from Cardiff University.

Why Vaccines Can Become Less Effective With Age

The COVID-19 pandemic reinforced the importance of vaccination in preventing severe disease and death. However, older adults often develop fewer protective antibodies and T cells after receiving a vaccine. Similar patterns have also been observed with influenza vaccination.

To investigate whether spermidine could improve this response, Simon and her colleagues enrolled 40 healthy adults aged 65 and older. After receiving their third COVID-19 vaccination, participants took either six milligrams of spermidine or a placebo each day for 13 weeks.

About one quarter of the participants produced very weak antibody responses even after three vaccine doses. Their immune cells also showed distinct signs of biological aging, including greater DNA damage and molecular markers linked to cellular senescence. During cellular senescence, damaged or aging cells stop working normally but remain in the body and accumulate over time.

Stronger Antibody Responses in Some Participants

Among the vaccine nonresponders who received spermidine, several indicators of vaccine-related immunity improved substantially. These participants generally developed higher levels of antibodies against SARS-CoV-2 and showed stronger neutralizing activity against several viral variants.

The researchers also found that spermidine lowered markers associated with immunosenescence and increased autophagy. Autophagy is a natural recycling process that allows cells to remove damaged material and maintain normal function.

The supplement appeared to be safe and well tolerated. Researchers found no adverse effects associated with the treatment.

Larger Trials Are Still Needed

Because the study included only 40 participants, the researchers caution that the results should be viewed as early evidence rather than definitive proof that spermidine can improve vaccination outcomes.

"This study was designed as a pilot trial and involved a relatively small number of participants," says Simon. "Larger studies will be needed to determine whether spermidine can consistently improve vaccine responses and whether similar effects are seen with other vaccines, such as those used against seasonal influenza."
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a promising scientific discovery that a natural compound, spermidine, may enhance vaccine efficacy in older adults by improving immune response, addressing a significant health challenge related to aging. The study has meaningful implications for public health, especially for the aging population, and includes detailed research context and results, though larger trials are needed to confirm findings.
articles.personality_title: Natural compound spermidine may boost vaccine response in older adults
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – As people age, their immune systems often become weaker, making vaccines less effective. This can leave older adults less protected from diseases like COVID-19 and the flu.

**What happened** – Researchers gave a natural compound called spermidine, found in foods like mushrooms and aged cheese, to 40 adults over 65 after their third COVID-19 vaccine dose. They took spermidine or a placebo daily for 13 weeks. The study found that those who initially had weak immune responses showed stronger protection after taking spermidine.

**Impact** – Spermidine helped improve antibody levels and the ability to fight different virus variants in older adults whose immune systems were aging. It also reduced signs of immune cell aging and boosted a natural cell-cleaning process called autophagy. The supplement was safe and well tolerated.

**What's next step** – Since this was a small study, larger trials are needed to confirm if spermidine consistently helps older adults respond better to vaccines. Researchers also want to see if it works with other vaccines, like the flu shot.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A natural compound in common foods shows early promise for helping older adults develop stronger immunity after vaccination by supporting aging immune cells.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le composé naturel spermidine pourrait améliorer la réponse vaccinale des personnes âgées
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – En vieillissant, le système immunitaire s’affaiblit, rendant les vaccins moins efficaces. Cela laisse les personnes âgées moins protégées contre des maladies comme la COVID-19 et la grippe.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont donné un composé naturel appelé spermidine, présent dans des aliments comme les champignons et le fromage affiné, à 40 adultes de plus de 65 ans après leur troisième dose de vaccin contre la COVID-19. Ils ont pris de la spermidine ou un placebo chaque jour pendant 13 semaines. L’étude a montré que ceux qui avaient une réponse immunitaire faible au départ avaient une meilleure protection après avoir pris la spermidine.

**Impact** – La spermidine a aidé à augmenter le niveau d’anticorps et la capacité à combattre plusieurs variants du virus chez les personnes âgées dont le système immunitaire vieillissait. Elle a aussi réduit les signes de vieillissement des cellules immunitaires et renforcé un processus naturel de nettoyage cellulaire appelé autophagie. Le complément était sûr et bien toléré.

**Étape suivante** – Comme cette étude est petite, des essais plus larges sont nécessaires pour confirmer si la spermidine aide vraiment les personnes âgées à mieux répondre aux vaccins. Les chercheurs veulent aussi savoir si cela fonctionne avec d’autres vaccins, comme celui contre la grippe.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un composé naturel présent dans des aliments courants montre un potentiel prometteur pour aider les personnes âgées à renforcer leur immunité après la vaccination en soutenant leurs cellules immunitaires vieillissantes.
articles.personality_title_es: El compuesto natural espermidina podría mejorar la respuesta a vacunas en adultos mayores
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – A medida que las personas envejecen, su sistema inmunológico se vuelve menos eficiente, lo que hace que las vacunas sean menos efectivas. Esto deja a los adultos mayores menos protegidos contra enfermedades como el COVID-19 y la gripe.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores dieron un compuesto natural llamado espermidina, que se encuentra en alimentos como champiñones y quesos añejos, a 40 adultos mayores de 65 años después de su tercera dosis de vacuna contra el COVID-19. Tomaron espermidina o un placebo diariamente durante 13 semanas. El estudio encontró que quienes tenían una respuesta inmune débil al principio mostraron una mejor protección tras tomar espermidina.

**Impacto** – La espermidina ayudó a mejorar los niveles de anticuerpos y la capacidad para combatir varias variantes del virus en adultos mayores con sistemas inmunológicos envejecidos. También redujo señales de envejecimiento celular inmunológico y aumentó un proceso natural de limpieza celular llamado autofagia. El suplemento fue seguro y bien tolerado.

**Próximo paso** – Debido a que este fue un estudio pequeño, se necesitan ensayos más grandes para confirmar si la espermidina ayuda de forma constante a los adultos mayores a responder mejor a las vacunas. Los investigadores también quieren ver si funciona con otras vacunas, como la de la gripe.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Un compuesto natural presente en alimentos comunes muestra un prometedor potencial para ayudar a los adultos mayores a fortalecer su inmunidad tras la vacunación apoyando sus células inmunes envejecidas.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a vibrant, glowing cluster of microscopic immune cells rejuvenated by swirling golden strands symbolizing spermidine, set against a soft background of aged, gently fading cellular structures being renewed through delicate, flowing light and subtle hints of wheat germ, mushrooms, and cheese shapes blending naturally into the scene.

articles.title: New hope in the fight against cachexia — cancer’s deadly co-conspirator
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 21 July 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02228-7Scientists are getting closer to understanding the wasting and fatigue that often accompanies cancer. Treatments could be on the horizon.
articles.content:
Mealtimes were always a family affair for the Kochanczyks of Indiana. Dave Kochanczyk loved barbecuing, cooking Polish fare and preparing fresh vegetables from the garden. When not in the kitchen, he led an active life: working as an electrical contractor, serving as a volunteer firefighter and managing the family’s large country property.

Cancer cachexia contender enters first pivotal trial

That all changed after he was diagnosed with pancreatic cancer in 2010. Over the next seven years, as he endured intensive treatment, his love of food remained but his appetite dwindled. He lost muscle mass and became gaunt. Weakened and fatigued, he could no longer chop wood for the home’s stove.

Kochanczyk’s struggles were not only the result of the tumour and treatments waging war against one another in his body, but also due to cachexia, a cancer-linked syndrome that causes reduced appetite and muscle wasting. “It’s very hard to watch,” recalls Dave’s son Martin Kochanczyk. Since his father’s death in 2017, Martin has become an advocate for cachexia awareness with the Cancer Cachexia Society, a community of scientists, people with the condition and others advancing research, awareness and treatment.

As Martin often explains to people with cancer and their families, his father was hardly an outlier. “Cachexia affects a lot of people,” he says. Between 50% and 80% of people with cancer, depending on the tumour type and disease stage, experience cachexia.

“I’ve heard people describe it as lying down under a thousand-pound blanket,” says sociologist Abigail Newell, senior director of research at the Cancer Support Community in Washington DC. “Sitting up feels like running a marathon.” And it’s more than a quality-of-life issue; cachexia affects survival. Exhaustion, hopelessness and other psychological symptoms can make people less willing to keep up with treatments. Severe cachexia can even make some therapies intolerable, or cause individuals to be excluded from clinical trials. Ultimately, 20–30% of cancer-associated deaths are attributable to cachexia, rather than the cancer itself.

Why are so many young people getting cancer? What researchers do and don’t know

Although cachexia was long considered an inescapable part of cancer’s progression, it has now become a topic of study in its own right. In 2020, a global research initiative, Cancer Grand Challenges, asked scientists to propose projects on cachexia.

The initiative ended up funding an international team of clinicians, scientists and advocates — 16 research groups across 14 institutions — to the tune of £20 million ($US25 million) to study cancer cachexia, its drivers, subtypes and potential treatments. Now in its fifth year, the project, known as CANCAN, has released about 30 papers, which have appeared alongside a rising tide of research from independent efforts.

With the influx of fresh studies, the very concept of cachexia is changing. Where scientists once saw simple malnutrition, muscle wasting and weight loss, they now see a whole-body syndrome of metabolism gone haywire. Studies reveal how cachexia is more than physical symptoms; it affects not just appetite and metabolism, but also motivation and behaviour. And a handful of companies, including pharmaceutical giant Pfizer, are testing medicines for cachexia (see ‘Fighting cachexia’), raising hope that a treatment might be on the horizon.

Source: Ref. 10

“It’s still an area of mystery,” says Ryan Schoenfeld, chief executive of the Mark Foundation for Cancer Research in New York City, a grant-making organization. But the burst of activity over the past few years has made him optimistic. “I feel confident in the future of the research here, that we’ll uncover ways to treat cachexia that will make it more reversible than it is today,” he says. “Ideally, we’ll prevent it.”

Organ crosstalk

Cachexia has long been something of a paradox in cancer: widely prevalent and with a huge impact on people, it nevertheless existed beyond the gaze of many physicians and scientists. Specialists typically focused on the tumour, reasoning that eliminating the cancer would relieve the cachexia, says Schoenfeld. There are no approved medications for cancer cachexia in most parts of the world. The appetite stimulant anamorelin is available in Japan, but the US Food and Drug Administration and the European Medicines Agency found the evidence for its benefits to be insufficient for approval. Even if people with the condition force themselves to eat more, or are fed through a tube, it doesn’t solve the problem.

With more people surviving for longer with cancer, the need for cachexia treatment is only growing, and funders and scientists have taken note. “There’s a whole ecosystem of cachexia researchers now,” says Tobias Janowitz, a physician-scientist at Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory in New York and co-principal investigator of CANCAN. Once a specialist topic relegated to niche conferences, cachexia has been on the programme of the annual meeting of the American Association for Cancer Research for the past several years, notes Mariam Jamal-Hanjani, a clinician-scientist at University College London and a CANCAN co-investigator.

Andrea Bonetto, a muscle physiologist at the University of Colorado Anschutz in Aurora, has been studying cachexia since before the field took off. When he began, the condition was considered mainly a case of muscle wasting. “What is now clear, compared to 24 years ago, is that cachexia is not a single gene, a single-tissue disease, but it’s multifaceted, it’s multi-tissue, it’s multi-systemic,” he says. “There’s huge crosstalk among different tissues and organs.” Each region of the body might receive signals that cause it to alter its metabolism or activities, and to send signals that influence cachexia pathways in other parts.

The path starts with the cancer. The immune system might respond to the presence of abnormal cells, resulting in inflammation. But cachexia-associated signalling spreads widely. Bonetto’s group found, for example, that tumours that neither originate in nor infiltrate bone can, nonetheless, cause cell death and breakdown in bones1. Antibodies that block a regulator of bone remodelling and muscle structure called RANKL, which is made by bone and by several tumour types, minimized bone and muscle loss and preserved strength in a mouse model of ovarian cancer2. Bisphosphonate drugs used to treat osteoporosis had similar benefits, suggesting that these medications might have a place in treating cachexia, too.

The liver also seems to have a key role in cachexia signalling. For one, its secretions seem to worsen the breakdown of muscle and bone, Bonetto says. A contribution from this organ makes sense, says Mauricio Berriel Diaz, a biologist at the Helmholtz Centre Munich in Germany, because the liver is a major regulator of metabolism. He and his colleagues reported that liver cells respond to cachexia by secreting a handful of specific compounds3. In lab dishes, these compounds cause heart cells to shrink and fat cells to break down fat. Levels of these secretions were also elevated in people with cachexia3.

Another study, in mouse models of cachexia, linked liver problems to the vagus nerve, which runs between the brain and other organs. Vagus dysfunction caused the liver to alter its metabolism, adopting a pro-inflammatory, cachexia-promoting state. Blocking vagal activity minimized weight loss and improved appetite and activity level in these mice4.

A disease of the brain

The brain ultimately becomes a key orchestrator of cachexia, says Adam Kepecs, a neuroscientist at Washington University in St. Louis, Missouri. It is the only place in the body that can integrate the various inflammatory, metabolic and hormonal elements of such a complex condition, Kepecs says. And it’s where cachexia becomes not just physical, but also psychological.

US cancer deaths are falling — but not fast enough

Right now, much of the excitement around drug development for cachexia has to do with the action of the stress hormone GDF15 in the brain. GDF15 activates a signalling pathway that diminishes appetite and creates a sense of satiety. If you eat something that doesn’t agree with you, GDF15 sounds the alarm, so you don’t eat it again. The hormone is a well-known contributor to the appetite loss seen in cachexia, but scientists don’t fully understand where it comes from or how it participates in the condition.

At the University of Oklahoma in Oklahoma City, cancer biologist Min Li and his colleagues have identified GDF15’s role in a three-way conversation between the tumour, the brain and the immune system. Tumour cells alone don’t cause much cachexia in the mice that he and his colleagues used, Li says. But they release signals that cause immune cells to produce GDF15. When GDF15 reaches the brain, the brain then signals back to the tumours, causing them to recruit further immune cells. These then become additional GDF15 producers, creating a vicious cycle. Eliminating or blocking GDF15 reduced cachexia in the mice5.

Immune signals and the inflammation they cause are well-known contributors to cachexia, but Kepecs and his colleagues found that at least one immune molecule has an influence well beyond affecting appetite and wasting. When the researchers did behavioural tests on mice with cachexia, they found that the animals were less likely to put in extra effort to obtain food or water — even beyond expectations for mice with low appetite and reduced energy levels6. They seemed to have low motivation.
articles.reasoning: The article reports significant progress in understanding and potentially treating cancer cachexia, a syndrome that severely affects many cancer patients' quality of life and survival. It highlights a major international research initiative with substantial funding, new scientific insights into the syndrome's mechanisms, and ongoing drug trials, all of which promise tangible benefits for a broad patient population. The story is focused, detailed, and addresses a critical unmet medical need with hopeful prospects for future treatment.
articles.personality_title: New research brings hope for treating cancer-related muscle loss
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Cancer cachexia is a condition that causes people with cancer to lose muscle and appetite, making them very weak and tired. It affects up to 80% of cancer patients and can even cause death, separate from the cancer itself. Until recently, doctors had few ways to treat this problem.

**What happened** – An international research project called CANCAN, funded with £20 million, has been studying cachexia to understand how it works and find treatments. Scientists discovered that cachexia is a complex condition involving many organs like muscles, bones, liver, and the brain. They found that signals between the tumor, immune system, and brain cause appetite loss and muscle wasting. Some drug companies, including Pfizer, are now testing medicines to help patients with cachexia.

**Impact** – This research changes how we think about cachexia—from just being about eating less to a whole-body disease that affects motivation and survival. The new understanding means treatments could target the different parts of the body involved, not just the tumor. This could improve the quality of life and chances of survival for many cancer patients.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue studying the signals that cause cachexia and testing new drugs in clinical trials. They hope to find medicines that can stop or reverse muscle loss and improve appetite. This could also help more patients stay strong enough to continue cancer treatments.

**One-sentence takeaway** – New discoveries about how cancer causes muscle loss are leading to promising treatments that could help many patients live stronger during their fight against cancer.
articles.personality_title_fr: De nouvelles recherches ouvrent la voie au traitement de la perte musculaire liée au cancer
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La cachexie cancéreuse est une maladie qui fait perdre du muscle et l'appétit aux personnes atteintes de cancer, les rendant très faibles et fatiguées. Elle touche jusqu'à 80 % des patients et peut causer la mort, indépendamment du cancer lui-même. Jusqu'à récemment, les médecins avaient peu de solutions pour traiter ce problème.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Un projet de recherche international appelé CANCAN, financé à hauteur de 20 millions de livres, étudie la cachexie pour mieux comprendre son fonctionnement et trouver des traitements. Les scientifiques ont découvert que la cachexie est une maladie complexe qui implique plusieurs organes comme les muscles, les os, le foie et le cerveau. Ils ont constaté que des signaux entre la tumeur, le système immunitaire et le cerveau causent la perte d'appétit et la fonte musculaire. Certaines entreprises pharmaceutiques, dont Pfizer, testent désormais des médicaments pour aider les patients.

**Impact** – Cette recherche change notre vision de la cachexie : ce n’est pas seulement une question de moindre alimentation, mais une maladie qui touche tout le corps, affectant la motivation et la survie. Cette nouvelle compréhension permet de cibler différents organes, pas seulement la tumeur, pour améliorer la vie et les chances des patients.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont continuer à étudier les signaux qui provoquent la cachexie et tester de nouveaux médicaments en essais cliniques. Ils espèrent trouver des traitements capables d’arrêter ou d’inverser la perte musculaire et d’améliorer l’appétit. Cela aidera aussi davantage de patients à rester assez forts pour suivre leurs traitements contre le cancer.

**Phrase clé** – De nouvelles découvertes sur la façon dont le cancer cause la perte musculaire ouvrent la voie à des traitements prometteurs pour aider de nombreux patients à rester plus forts durant leur combat contre la maladie.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevas investigaciones abren esperanza para tratar la pérdida muscular relacionada con el cáncer
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La caquexia cancerosa es una condición que causa que las personas con cáncer pierdan músculo y apetito, volviéndose muy débiles y cansadas. Afecta hasta al 80% de los pacientes y puede causar la muerte, aparte del cáncer mismo. Hasta hace poco, los médicos tenían pocas formas de tratar este problema.

**Qué pasó** – Un proyecto internacional llamado CANCAN, financiado con 20 millones de libras, ha estado estudiando la caquexia para entender cómo funciona y buscar tratamientos. Los científicos descubrieron que la caquexia es una enfermedad compleja que involucra muchos órganos como músculos, huesos, hígado y cerebro. Encontraron que señales entre el tumor, el sistema inmune y el cerebro causan pérdida de apetito y desgaste muscular. Algunas compañías farmacéuticas, incluyendo Pfizer, están probando medicamentos para ayudar a los pacientes.

**Impacto** – Esta investigación cambia la forma en que vemos la caquexia: no es solo comer menos, sino una enfermedad de todo el cuerpo que afecta la motivación y la supervivencia. Este nuevo entendimiento puede llevar a tratamientos que atacan diferentes partes del cuerpo, no solo el tumor. Esto podría mejorar la calidad de vida y las posibilidades de sobrevivir para muchos pacientes.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán estudiando las señales que causan la caquexia y probando nuevos medicamentos en ensayos clínicos. Esperan encontrar medicinas que puedan detener o revertir la pérdida muscular y mejorar el apetito. Esto también ayudará a que más pacientes se mantengan lo suficientemente fuertes para continuar sus tratamientos contra el cáncer.

**Frase clave** – Nuevos descubrimientos sobre cómo el cáncer causa pérdida muscular están llevando a tratamientos prometedores que podrían ayudar a muchos pacientes a mantenerse más fuertes durante su lucha contra el cáncer.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a sturdy, glowing heart-shaped lantern illuminating a winding path surrounded by intertwined roots and branches symbolizing organs and nerves, with a vibrant, hopeful sunrise breaking through soft mist, all rendered in gentle natural earth tones.
